title: The Ordeal of Democracy in the Tamil Nadu Legislative Assembly!
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement.
paragraphs: On 21 July 2014, members of the Dravida Munnetra Kazhagam walked out of the Tamil Nadu Legislative Assembly twice. Do you know why? The opposition, and DMK members in particular, are not allowed to speak in the Assembly about issues affecting the welfare of their constituencies. The moment they begin to point out a failing or make an accusation against the ruling party, the Chief Minister and the ministers concerned rise, consume all the time allotted to the member, and say whatever comes to mind in a manner that wounds the opposition. If DMK members answer them according to the rules of the House, their replies are arbitrarily expunged from the record. In the past, after Question Hour, adjournment motions, calling-attention motions and special calling-attention motions submitted by opposition parties were taken up. Members presented their views, and ministers replied afterwards. That was the established practice. Is it followed now?

What is happening in Tamil Nadu? Shall I speak only of what happened in a single day yesterday? Is law and order being protected? Sand theft has crossed every limit across Tamil Nadu. On 20 July, after Takolam police station received information about illegal sand quarrying near Arakkonam in Vellore district, Sub-Inspector Rajan and Head Constable Kanagaraj went there. Sand was being taken in a tractor. When Kanagaraj tried to stop it, the tractor driver mercilessly pushed him down, drove the vehicle over him and killed him.

This year's National Crime Records Bureau report contains extensive information about crimes against women and the rise in sexual offences in Tamil Nadu. The DMK submitted a calling-attention motion for an Assembly debate. Reports say that Karnataka's Congress government suffered embarrassment over increasing sexual assaults and that its Chief Minister planned to remove the Home Minister after receiving Sonia Gandhi's approval. What will the AIADMK government do about rising sexual offences in Tamil Nadu? Will it follow Karnataka's example, or close its eyes and pretend that nothing has happened?

Jayalalithaa held a grand felicitation in Thanjavur and accepted a title and prize, claiming credit after the Cauvery tribunal's final award was published in the Union Gazette under a Supreme Court order. Yet the Cauvery Management Board required by that award has still not been constituted. Delta farmers staged a siege demanding the Board. Police brutally attacked them, and the AIADMK government arrested thousands. Should this double standard not be debated in the Assembly?

Akbar Ali, the Central Chennai district president of the Tamil State Muslim League, was speaking with friends when a gang suddenly arrived and hacked him to death with sickles. This happened in Tamil Nadu's capital.

For two months there have been reports of leopards roaming near Chengalpattu outside Chennai. People are terrified. Women who saw one fled in panic, and cameras have recorded the animals. Should the Assembly not debate what action the government has taken?

There was a report that a woman software engineer was abducted in a car in an attempted sexual assault. Another reported that a gang promising thirty lakh rupees for a ten-lakh investment had been arrested for online fraud.
A five-year-old girl was sexually harassed aboard the Nellai Express.

Vairamani, an assistant executive engineer at the North Chennai Thermal Power Station, retired in June 2012. His retirement benefits were not paid. A photograph in yesterday's news showed him fasting after 127 petitions to the Chief Minister's Special Cell produced no result.

Newspapers reported that at Pattaravakkam in Ambattur, an AIADMK youth-wing leader stabbed to death a young man named John, who had stopped and argued with the leader's relatives, and also stabbed four people who intervened. Should this not be discussed in the Assembly?

Students in government and aided schools in Tirunelveli district staged a sit-in at the Collector's office because laptops had not been supplied to Computer Science and self-financing stream students. Police assaulted and dispersed them.
The Tamil Nadu Farmers' Association demonstrated in Salem yesterday, demanding a higher milk procurement price.

Although sand quarrying was not permitted in the Pennaiyar near Panruti, hundreds of lorry-loads were allegedly being removed daily with giant machines. Officials took no action despite repeated reports. Yesterday, three hundred villagers detained four hundred parked lorries and staged a siege. Sand smuggling allegedly continued through payments to officials despite a court ban. Should this not be debated in the Assembly?

Inflow into Mettur has risen to 23,000 cubic feet and the level has reached 56 feet. With the water continuing to rise, should we not ask, in the interest of farmers' livelihoods, when the dam will be opened for irrigation?

Yesterday's newspaper also reported that 250 people travelled in a government bus at Attur near Salem and three schoolgirls fainted in the crush. It said people were suffering because too few buses were being operated.

All these reports appeared in the newspapers on just one day. Is the Assembly not meant for discussing such successive problems that directly affect Tamil Nadu's people? Is it there only to sing praises of the Chief Minister? The Assembly met on 10 July. It has sat for only nine days. How many walkouts and expulsions have already occurred?

When the eleven-storey building at Moulivakkam collapsed, killing 61 people, opposition parties sought permission on 10 July to debate it. The Speaker refused. Leaders of the DMK, DMDK, Congress, CPI(M), CPI, Puthiya Tamilagam and Manithaneya Makkal Katchi asked that their adjournment motion be taken up, but permission was denied because an inquiry commission had been formed. As the opposition raised the demand, the minister concerned angrily asked whether they had the courage to hear his reply. Is this Jayalalithaa's Assembly?

Although the Leader of the House said one member from each party could speak, the Speaker refused permission. Only then did every opposition party walk out. Yet the minister later gave a lengthy explanation of the building collapse during his reply. If an inquiry commission prevented debate, could the minister alone speak? Referring to the opposition's democratic walkout against his arrogant remarks, he said, "The deserters have run away." Is this Jayalalithaa's Assembly?

On the eleventh sitting, opposition members asked that "deserters" be expunged from the record. The minister insisted he had said nothing wrong, and the Speaker ruled that it was not an improper word. The Chief Minister reportedly sat smiling, perhaps delighted by her minister's refinement. When DMK members went before the Speaker's chair and sought removal of the unparliamentary expression, he said, in effect, "Watch me throw all of you out," and expelled them on only the second day of the session. Is this Jayalalithaa's Legislative Assembly?

On 15 July, during the Social Welfare grant debate, DMK member Pushpaleela Alban listed achievements under the DMK government. The minister and Chief Minister interrupted to say that I had lain in hospital during the tsunami. Was that necessary? My health had deteriorated two days before the tsunami, during the AIADMK government. Can one enter hospital without illness, especially under Jayalalithaa's rule? When such an accusation was made, should our members not have been allowed to answer? Was the Speaker's ruling that no one could speak after the Chief Minister the final word? DMK members walked out in protest.

On 17 July, permission was denied to take up adjournment and calling-attention motions on drought, severe drinking-water scarcity and price rises, prompting a walkout. The DMDK, CPI(M), CPI and Puthiya Tamilagam also walked out over the same issues. Later that day, after a ruling-party member spoke about me in an indecent and repulsive manner and DMK members protested, every DMK member was expelled.

On 18 July, while reading a statement under Rule 110, the Chief Minister unnecessarily accused me. When brother Duraimurugan sought an opportunity to answer and was denied it, DMK members walked out. They walked out twice more that day for the same reason.

On 21 July, DMK members walked out when permission was denied to debate crimes against women during the AIADMK's three years in office. They walked out again during the Electricity grant debate when the government claimed credit for power projects introduced under the DMK government.

On 22 July, a minister once again demeaned me by dragging up the old claim that I was in hospital during the tsunami. When DMK members protested, they were expelled for the third time. In only nine sitting days since 10 July, they walked out eight times and were expelled three times. Because they had supposedly been expelled four times during this session, they were barred from all remaining proceedings. What a magnificent democracy flies its flag in the Tamil Nadu Assembly!

All this was discussed in detail at yesterday's meeting of DMK legislators. To condemn the AIADMK government's conduct, the DMK resolved to hold protest meetings titled "The Ordeal of Democracy in the Tamil Nadu Legislative Assembly" in district headquarters and other major towns. I and all DMK legislators will attend the Chennai meeting on 31 July. Meetings in the remaining towns will be held on 1 and 2 August, with DMK legislators and speakers participating. They will explain in detail how democracy is being disregarded in the Assembly. I ask every Udanpirappu to make these protest meetings a success, as always, so that the dignity of the Assembly may be protected and authoritarian impulses checked.
With affection,
M. K.
24 July 2014
